attributes.stem:
A 24-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of severe headache, neck stiffness, and confusion. He returned one week ago from working on a cattle station in the Northern Territory during the wet season. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 130/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.9°C. He is drowsy but rousable. There is marked neck stiffness and photophobia. Neurological examination reveals no focal deficits.

Brain magnetic resonance imaging shows bilateral thalamic changes. Murray Valley encephalitis virus PCR from cerebrospinal fluid returns positive.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following cerebrospinal fluid findings is most likely to be present?
attributes.explanation:
This case presents a confirmed case of Murray Valley encephalitis virus infection, with typical features including recent travel to an endemic area during the wet season, progressive neurological symptoms, and characteristic bilateral thalamic changes on imaging.

Lymphocytic pleocytosis is the characteristic CSF finding in viral encephalitis, including Murray Valley encephalitis. The CSF typically shows a mild to moderate increase in lymphocytes (usually 10-300 cells/µL) with normal glucose levels and mildly elevated protein.

Neutrophilic pleocytosis is more typical of bacterial meningitis, where neutrophils predominate and glucose is often decreased due to bacterial consumption.

Xanthochromia refers to yellow discolouration of CSF, typically seen in subarachnoid haemorrhage after red blood cell breakdown, and would not be expected in viral encephalitis.

Low glucose only would be unusual in isolation and is more commonly associated with bacterial or fungal infections rather than viral causes.

Normal cell count would be inconsistent with the inflammatory process occurring in viral encephalitis.

Murray Valley encephalitis virus is transmitted by Culex annulirostris mosquitoes and is endemic to northern Australia and Papua New Guinea. The diagnosis is typically confirmed by serology or PCR of CSF. Treatment is supportive, and prevention through mosquito avoidance is crucial.
learningPoints: Viral encephalitis typically shows lymphocytic pleocytosis in CSF analysis.
CSF protein is usually elevated while glucose remains normal in viral encephalitis.
Murray Valley encephalitis is transmitted by Culex mosquitoes and is endemic to northern Australia and Papua New Guinea.
references: Knox J, Cowan RU, Doyle JS, et al. Murray Valley encephalitis: a review of clinical features, diagnosis and treatment. Med J Aust. 2012;196(5):322-326. doi:10.5694/mja11.11026
